title: Israel's next government must prioritize the Abraham Accords
gist: Israel's October 2026 election, framed as a referendum on the failures around the October 7 Hamas attack, could open a narrow window to revive the near-frozen Abraham Accords, but doing so requires restoring Gulf credibility, deepening institutionalized US coordination, re-engaging the Palestinian track, and operationalizing existing minilateral frameworks like I2U2 and IMEC.

themes.theme: Israel's fragile political landscape ahead of the October 2026 election

insights.claim: By the end of 2025, only about a quarter of Israelis expressed confidence in their government, while around three-quarters supported establishing a state commission of inquiry into the failures surrounding the October 7 Hamas attack.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Signals deep, durable public distrust in the Netanyahu government that underlies electoral volatility and could accelerate a leadership change relevant to Israeli policy risk.
insights.locator: The Israeli public has shown declining trust

insights.claim: Recent polls suggest Prime Minister Netanyahu's Likud party would secure an estimated twenty-four seats in the 120-member Knesset, down from the thirty-two it currently holds, with the existing coalition well below the sixty-one-seat majority threshold.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Quantifies a material erosion in Netanyahu's electoral base, raising the probability of a coalition change in the October 2026 election that would matter for regional-normalization policy continuity.
insights.locator: Recent polls suggest Netanyahu's Likud party

insights.claim: The unexpected political reunion of former prime ministers Naftali Bennett and Yair Lapid this spring is the opening development in a heated fall 2026 election campaign, with Bennett running as a centrist who has shown openness to working with regional allies and has met the UAE president.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Identifies Bennett as the most likely alternative to Netanyahu with a track record more favorable to regional integration, a specific figure to watch for a policy pivot on the Abraham Accords.
insights.locator: Bennett is one of the most likely alternatives

themes.theme: Why the Abraham Accords stalled after October 7

insights.claim: The war in Gaza has driven Arab-world support for relations with Israel to a single-digit low, and although a ceasefire currently holds, Gaza remains politically unresolved with Hamas in de facto control and no viable alternative governing group.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Establishes the scale of the Arab public-opinion headwind that any normalization push must overcome, a key constraint for pricing the probability of accord expansion.
insights.locator: The war has fueled widespread anger

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia's position hardened from Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman's pre-October 2023 statement that 'every day we get closer' to normalization, to his late-2024 declaration that the kingdom 'will not establish diplomatic relations with Israel' without creation of an independent Palestinian state — a shift from a flexible negotiating framework to a fixed public condition.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Marks the single clearest bellwether for Saudi-Israel normalization: watch for any softening of MBS's public statehood condition as the key leading indicator of Accords expansion.
insights.quote: will not establish diplomatic relations with Israel

insights.claim: UAE envoy Lana Zaki Nusseibeh warned that Israeli actions risk undermining the 'vision and spirit' of the Abraham Accords, reflecting growing dissatisfaction among existing Accords partners with Israel's military conduct and West Bank policies despite continued security coordination.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Indicates existing Accords partners, not just prospective ones like Saudi Arabia, are at risk of disengaging, meaning normalization retrenchment is a live risk, not just stalled expansion.
insights.locator: As United Arab Emirates envoy Lana Zaki Nusseibeh warned

insights.claim: Indonesia, another prominent prospective Abraham Accords member, has similarly reiterated that progress on the Palestinian issue is a prerequisite for normalizing ties with Israel.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Confirms the Palestinian-statehood precondition is not Saudi-specific but a broader bloc requirement among the largest prospective new entrants, raising the bar for any expansion.

insights.claim: A May 2025 survey found 61 percent of Israelis support normalizing relations with Saudi Arabia and expanding the Abraham Accords, with two-thirds agreeing it would strengthen Israel's security, economy, and regional standing — revealing a gap between Israeli public appetite and the political reality of the region.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Domestic Israeli political demand for normalization is strong and not the binding constraint — the constraint is external (Gulf/Arab conditions) and Netanyahu's coalition limits, useful for separating demand-side from supply-side blockers.
insights.locator: The Israeli public, however, remains supportive

themes.theme: Four policy priorities for the next Israeli government

insights.claim: Israel previously showed the annexation-for-normalization trade-off works: in 2020 it agreed to shelve West Bank annexation plans in exchange for normalization with the UAE, and the author argues a new government must again shelve annexation discussions and curb settler violence to restore Gulf credibility.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, historically-proven policy lever (annexation restraint) as the near-term precondition for any renewed Gulf normalization progress.

insights.claim: The author recommends establishing a jointly US-Israel coordinated Abraham Accords directorate to institutionalize the framework beyond political cycles, covering regular regional forums, encouraging additional country accession, coordinating IMEC, and sustaining bipartisan US support tied to broader US interests like countering adversarial influence and expanding trade.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A concrete institutional proposal (a dedicated bilateral directorate) to watch for as evidence the Accords framework is being durably institutionalized rather than left to depend on individual administrations.

insights.claim: Full Palestinian statehood remains politically unlikely given strong Israeli public opposition and the current Palestinian Authority's incompetence as a governing partner, so the author recommends a narrow, incremental approach: implementing the ceasefire's second stage, expanding non-Hamas Palestinian technocrats' role in reconstruction, and strengthening PA revenue streams to prevent financial collapse.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Sets realistic near-term markers (PA revenue stabilization, technocrat inclusion) to track progress on the Palestinian track rather than an unrealistic full-statehood benchmark.

insights.claim: The next government should operationalize existing minilateral frameworks — expanding the UAE free trade agreement, advancing sector partnerships with Bahrain and Morocco, deepening the I2U2 Group (Israel, US, India, UAE), and accelerating the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC), which has grown more important amid Iranian and Houthi disruptions to traditional trade routes.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Identifies specific existing trade/investment vehicles (I2U2, IMEC, UAE FTA) that could see accelerated activity as tangible signals of Accords momentum even absent new country accessions.
